content_en:
If any single chapter encapsulates the urgency and anxiety that the pious predecessors felt regarding the preservation of Tawhid, it is this one. The chapter on the fear of shirk is not a chapter about pagans or distant unbelievers — it is a warning addressed directly to the hearts of those who already believe, because the greatest scholars of this ummah themselves feared falling into shirk without even being aware of it.

The opening text is the supplication of Ibrahim al-Khalil (Abraham, the intimate friend of Allah), recorded in the Quran: 'And keep me and my sons away from worshipping idols. My Lord, they have indeed led many people astray' (Quran 14:35-36). The fact that Ibrahim — described by Allah as a 'hanif' and a 'muslim', a man whose heart was completely surrendered to Allah — made this supplication is deeply instructive. If the father of the prophets, the one whom Allah called His intimate friend (Khalil), felt the need to seek Allah's protection from shirk for himself and his descendants, then what is the state of those far lesser in piety and closeness to Allah?

The chapter continues with a hadith from al-Bukhari, narrated by Ibrahim al-Taymi, who said: 'Who is safe from trials after Ibrahim?' This statement reflects the deeply held Salafi conviction that no one — regardless of their level of piety or knowledge — can be complacent about the threat of shirk. The great scholar Ibn Abi Hatim further recorded a statement attributed to Ibrahim al-Khalil himself, expressing that no one should feel secure from being tested through shirk.

Perhaps the most sobering quotation in this chapter comes from Ibrahim al-Nakha'i, one of the foremost scholars of the Tabi'un (the generation after the Companions), who said: 'If I were told that one of the people of this city had an ant's weight of hypocrisy in his heart, I would not fear that for them as much as I fear that for myself.' The great scholars recognized that the subtle pathways of shirk — showing off in worship (riya'), seeking reputation, performing acts of obedience for worldly gain — were more dangerous than the obvious shirk of idol worship, precisely because they are invisible and difficult to detect.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab uses this chapter to draw the reader's attention to what classical scholars called al-shirk al-asghar (minor shirk). This includes riya' (ostentation in worship), swearing by other than Allah, saying 'whatever Allah wills and whatever so-and-so wills', and other subtle expressions that attribute a share of sovereignty or will to other than Allah. The Prophet ﷺ famously said: 'The thing I fear most for you is al-shirk al-asghar.' When the Companions asked what that was, he said: 'Showing off (riya'). On the Day of Judgment, Allah will say to those who showed off: Go to those for whom you performed your deeds in this world, and see if you find any reward with them.' (Recorded by Ahmad and others.)

The message of this chapter is that fear of shirk is not paranoia — it is wisdom. The believer should hold their worship to a high standard of sincerity, regularly examining their intentions, and never assume that their Tawhid is so settled that it requires no vigilance. This ongoing self-examination is not anxiety about one's status before Allah but rather the mark of a heart that understands the weight of what Tawhid demands.
